source_title: Secret History #6:  The Psychology of Evil (Graphic and Disturbing, Viewer Discretion Advised)

interactions.summary: Jiang reviews the prior Secret History argument: Western religion moved from mother goddess to polytheism to monotheism, and the modern scientific classroom reverses the older view that mind precedes matter.
He frames the earliest mass societies as experiments in social control: China uses bureaucracy, the Indus Valley uses proto-Buddhist religion, Mesopotamia uses war, and Egypt uses the god-pharaoh.

Jiang prompts the class to name ordinary leadership virtues, receives short shouted answers, then rejects the school answer and says historical leaders need unpredictability, stress tolerance, and lack of empathy.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang prompt plus brief student answer fragments; not a substantive audience question.

interactions.summary: He condenses the three leadership traits into dissociation, then connects dissociation to multiple identities, strategic unpredictability, and game-theoretic cheating through secret alliances.

Jiang argues that secret alliances force elites into multiple identities: to belong to several powerful groups without revealing true loyalty, a person must be able to dissociate and become different selves in different settings.
He retells Egyptian mythology around Ra, Osiris, Horus, Seth, Isis, and Apophis, emphasizing violent ordeals rather than story coherence.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, including sensitive mythic violence described as lecture content.

interactions.summary: Jiang converts Egyptian myth into a script: because the pharaoh is a reincarnation of the gods, ritual can implant god-memories; Kant, Monkey Island, story, ritual, and psychedelics explain how experience can program identity.

Jiang spells out the alleged programming mechanism: rituals make the pharaoh identify as Ra, Osiris, or Horus; trauma creates dissociation; an Isis-masked priest becomes the trusted controller; and scent can trigger identities.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture; sensitive ritual violence is presented as his speculative reconstruction.

interactions.summary: He generalizes from the pharaoh to ordinary identity: school, home, friendship, and work each activate a different self; muscle memory shows that training can survive conscious amnesia.
He pushes the programming claim to violent extremes, says Egypt allegedly used the same techniques still used today, and repeatedly warns students to doubt him because the Egyptian evidence is lost.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture with explicit speculation disclaimer.

interactions.summary: A student asks whether only the pharaoh was trained this way, rather than relatives such as brothers.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question relayed after a microphone prompt.

interactions.summary: Jiang answers the student question by saying the whole royal family, not only the pharaoh, would be trained because priests need replacements and can weaponize factions through rival siblings.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang answer to the student question from the previous packet.

interactions.summary: He explains why the royal family would accept the ordeals: trauma, psychedelics, and near-death experiences allegedly give access to the spirit world and make them feel godlike.
A student asks whether near-death contact with the spirit world erases the distinction between heaven and hell; Jiang answers with the life review, where good and evil become felt directly.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer.

interactions.summary: Jiang returns to the main argument: priests control pharaohs as puppets, dissociative identity disorder is misery rather than freedom, and power tries to reduce its misery by making others miserable.
Jiang moves from Egypt to Abu Ghraib and enhanced interrogation, rejecting the rogue-soldier explanation and saying the program was designed by psychologists working for the CIA.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture about modern torture; claims are his presentation of the source material.

interactions.summary: He maps learned helplessness onto the Egyptian identities: the intended experiment is to turn Ra into Osiris, but he argues torture instead radicalizes prisoners and produces ISIS-like fighters.
Jiang compares Abu Zubaydah's reported torture to the Egyptian ritual script and says the real goal was not information but programming prisoners into weapons.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture; ASR is noisy around names and transitions.

interactions.summary: He extends the modern continuity claim to MK Ultra, describing drugs, hypnosis, interrogation, foreign sites, destroyed records, and social control as the underlying goal.

Jiang claims MK Ultra's results spread through society, connecting drug regimes, social media, psychology, and mass education to the same social-control logic; he closes with the takeaway that modern people are now the pharaoh.

A student asks whether social media should be government-controlled if it is a mechanism of social control; Jiang answers that the public platforms are private faces on an infrastructure built, protected, and controlled by the US military.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer.

interactions.summary: Jiang ends by disclaiming certainty: the Egyptian theory is speculative, unsupported by direct evidence, and students should take it skeptically.

speaker_notes.note: The class answer sequence is fragmentary and should not be surfaced as a public question; it is Jiang eliciting examples.
The substantive audience question is the training-scope question; the 'speak in the microphone' line is just a facilitation prompt.
The heaven/hell question is a real student question and is answered in segments 0029-0030.
This is a real student question about whether social media should be controlled by government; it has a direct answer in segment 0046.

claims.claim: Secret societies' weapon, in this lecture, is mind control: turning people into programmable robots that can be used as weapons.

Jiang contrasts an older worldview where mind precedes matter, metaphor reveals unseen forces, and intuition is trusted with a modern science-class worldview where brain creates mind, only measurable things count, and tools override intuition.

Mass society's central problem is social control, and Jiang assigns different control solutions to the earliest civilizations: bureaucracy in China, religion in the Indus Valley, war in Mesopotamia, and Pharaoh in Egypt.
The school list of leadership virtues is inverted: Jiang says effective historical leaders share unpredictability, high stress tolerance, and lack of empathy.
Dissociation is defined as the mind leaving identification with the body, making the person feel like an observer in a movie and producing unpredictability, stress tolerance, and lack of empathy.
In Jiang's game-theory frame, winning a mass game requires cheating through secret alliances, but because alliances police loyalty, the winning player needs multiple identities.
To win among secret alliances, a person must occupy the intersection of several powerful groups, and multiple personalities prevent any one group from knowing what the person really thinks.
Jiang says the Egyptian priestly problem is whether a born pharaoh can be trained into multiple personalities; he answers yes, while repeatedly marking the reconstruction as speculative.
Egyptian mythology is framed as unique because its stories center on Ra the life-giving sun god, Osiris the civilizing god, and Horus the stabilizer of kingship.
The Ra, Osiris, and Horus myths are interpreted not as stories to believe but as scripts to act out, because the pharaoh is treated as the literal reincarnation of those gods.
Using Kant, Jiang argues that controlling experience and memory can control perceived reality, because people only know phenomena and cannot directly know the thing-in-itself.
Monkey Island becomes an inheritance model: later generations can inherit formative experience through story, ritual, and psychedelics that make the story feel personally lived.
Ra, Osiris, and Horus are interpreted as three identities for the pharaoh: virtuous hero, passive victim, and vengeful child.
The alleged Egyptian ritual system creates trauma, trauma creates dissociation, and dissociation lets priests program the pharaoh.
The Isis figure becomes a controller because the traumatized pharaoh associates that voice and mask with support and trust.
Smell is presented as a primary control mechanism that can activate a particular identity and emotion in the pharaoh during decision-making.
Ordinary humans are programmable to a degree because settings activate different identities and behaviors: student, child, friend, employee.
Muscle memory is used as evidence that training can remain in the subconscious even if conscious identity and memories disappear.
Jiang explicitly states that the Egyptian programming reconstruction is speculative, should be doubted, and cannot be directly verified because the records are lost.
The lecture claims the same ritual techniques named in the Egyptian story are still used today and will be shown later in the lecture.
The alleged programming system trains the whole royal family because priests need replacement rulers and can program rival brothers to create civil war.
Jiang says the priests are analogous to what is now called the deep state: the class that must remain uncontrolled because it controls the ruler.
Near-death experiences and psychedelics are presented as the payoff for the royal family and priests because they give access to the spirit world, God, wisdom, and a feeling of being truly divine.
Jiang answers the heaven/hell question by defining hell as the life review in which a bad person finally feels the pain they caused others, while heaven is seeing the good one did.
A second theory offered in the answer is that love is a physical force; after death, more love allows higher ascent, while less love traps the person in return.
Dissociative personality disorder is described as miserable rather than empowering, and Jiang claims people in power reduce misery by making others more miserable.
Jiang says the Abu Ghraib torture was not random rogue behavior but a program designed by psychologists paid by the CIA.
Enhanced interrogation is interpreted as an experiment in learned helplessness intended to transform a Ra-like fighter into an Osiris-like passive victim.
Jiang links learned helplessness and positive psychology through Martin Seligman and provocatively calls school-taught positive psychology brainwashing.
The lecture argues that torture radicalized prisoners rather than pacifying them, connecting US-controlled prisons and torture to ISIS recruitment and extremism.
Jiang claims Abu Zubaydah's reported torture repeats the Egyptian script of force-feeding, strangling, coffin confinement, drowning, cutting, and sexual humiliation.
He says the goal of torture is not to extract information but to turn people into programmable secret weapons.
MK Ultra is presented as a 1950s and 1960s CIA program to test drugs, hypnosis, interrogation, and brainwashing on innocent citizens and through foreign intelligence settings.
Jiang says MK Ultra worked but its effectiveness was hidden, and its results spread through society in social media and drug-based psychological treatment.
The lecture's modern social-control takeaway is that everyone is now the pharaoh: contemporary mass media, mass education, and mass psychology allegedly control people by the same structure.

Jiang criticizes psychology by arguing psychologists have elevated rates of mental illness and that he personally has not seen people get better after seeing psychologists, while acknowledging others may have benefited.
claims.temporal_scope: Lecture claim and personal observation on 2025-09-11.

claims.claim: In response to the social-media question, Jiang says Twitter and Facebook are private public faces on top of internet infrastructure built, controlled, and protected by the US military.
Jiang explicitly frames the class as speculation and theory, not proven truth, and tells students to be skeptical and doubtful.

signature_moments.moment: The universe is one giant mind that vibrates, and matter appears where the vibrations intersect.
signature_moments.source_phrase: one giant mind that vibrates
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It supplies the metaphysical background for why modern materialist schooling is treated as a perceptual reversal.

signature_moments.moment: The first states are not mainly economies or cultures; they are four competing answers to the problem of getting a mass population under control.
signature_moments.source_phrase: your biggest problem is social control
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns civilization into a control-technology comparison.

signature_moments.moment: The virtues school praises are declared wrong; the real leader is unpredictable, unbothered, and without empathy.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Guess what, guys? Wrong
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the lecture's first hard moral inversion.

signature_moments.moment: Dissociation makes you both inside the movie and watching the movie.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You are in the movie, but you're also watching the movie
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the abstract psychological claim a portable image.

signature_moments.moment: The winner is not merely in a secret society; he is the intersection of many secret societies.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You have to be the intersection of these groups
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns dissociation into a political navigation technology.

signature_moments.moment: Egyptian mythology starts to make sense only when treated as something to act out rather than something to believe.
signature_moments.source_phrase: It's not something to be believed. It's something to be acted out
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the pivot from myth summary to programming machinery.

signature_moments.moment: A story plus psychedelics can make descendants feel they survived Monkey Island themselves.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you actually believe the story is happening to you
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains how Jiang thinks ritual transfers memory, not just values.

signature_moments.moment: Ra, Osiris, and Horus stop being gods and become installed identities: hero, victim, and vengeful child.
signature_moments.source_phrase: represent different identities
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture's central decoding of Egyptian myth.

signature_moments.moment: Isis becomes the trusted controller, whispering commands to the pharaoh through the mask and voice that once calmed him.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Isis becomes the controller
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns consolation into the command channel.

signature_moments.moment: A scent in the throne room can change which self is making the decision.
signature_moments.source_phrase: different scents for different identities
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The political mechanism becomes sensory and concrete.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang keeps the theory hot but places a warning label on it: doubt me, because the records are gone.
signature_moments.source_phrase: doubt and question me
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It marks the speculative status of the most extreme reconstruction.

signature_moments.moment: The pharaoh's brother becomes a programmable civil-war cartridge when the priest class splits into factions.
signature_moments.source_phrase: program the pharaoh's brother to create a civil war
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows the political risk of programming a whole ruling family.

signature_moments.moment: The ordeal is sold as death without staying dead: a temporary passage into the spirit world where God is felt as love.
signature_moments.source_phrase: for like a minute or 10 minutes or half an hour they are literally dead
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why a brutal ritual could appear desirable from within the model.

signature_moments.moment: Hell is not a place but the sudden return of pain to a person who used dissociation to avoid feeling it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you have to feel their pain
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It links afterlife ethics directly back to dissociation.

signature_moments.moment: The dissociated ruler can only reduce misery by manufacturing more misery outside himself.
signature_moments.source_phrase: making others more miserable than you are
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture's dark psychology of evil.

signature_moments.moment: Learned helplessness becomes an attempt to turn Ra into Osiris: the fighter into the tortured victim.
signature_moments.source_phrase: turn Ra into Osiris
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It fuses the Egyptian myth frame with War on Terror psychology.

signature_moments.moment: The intended conversion reverses: instead of Ra becoming Osiris, Osiris becomes Ra.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Osiris became Ra
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is Jiang's account of why torture breeds the thing it claims to suppress.

signature_moments.moment: The interrogation room is recast as a drone factory for human weapons.
signature_moments.source_phrase: turn these people into drones
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves the lecture's hardest modern analogy.

signature_moments.moment: What was done to the pharaoh becomes a general technology: drugs, torture, ritual abuse, hypnosis, and mind control.
signature_moments.source_phrase: now they figure out how to do this to anyone
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns the ancient reconstruction into a modern mass-control claim.

signature_moments.moment: The punchline of the lecture: good news, you're now the pharaoh.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you're now the pharaoh
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes ancient ruler-programming a mass modern condition.

signature_moments.moment: The way they controlled the pharaoh 5,000 years ago becomes the way mass media and mass education control people now.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the way that they control us today
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the lecture's final ancient-to-modern compression.

signature_moments.moment: Twitter and Facebook become the public face; the real body is the military internet infrastructure underneath.
signature_moments.source_phrase: that's the public face
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It answers the student question by shifting ownership from apps to infrastructure.

signature_moments.moment: After the strongest claims, Jiang closes by saying he has no evidence that the Egyptian theory is true.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I have no evidence that it's true
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The disclaimer is essential provenance for the public read.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A state where Jiang says the mind stops identifying with the body and experiences events as happening to someone else.
The central problem of mass society: controlling the population and making people get along.
China's civil service examination, presented as the bureaucratic mechanism that stabilizes society and survives as gaokao.
Jiang's alternate phrase for dissociation and strategic multi-identity behavior.
Jiang's interpretive move in which Egyptian myth is read as a ritual program to enact, not a belief narrative.
ASR rendering of Kant's noumena, the things-in-themselves that Jiang contrasts with phenomena.
The world as experienced or seen, which Jiang says can be controlled by controlling memory and experience.
The process of using ritualized experience to install memory, identity, and behavior.
The trusted support figure who can later activate or command the programmed person.
A sensory cue, especially smell in this lecture, that activates an identity and its emotions.
Training stored in the subconscious, used as the analogy for programmed behavior surviving conscious amnesia.
An ordeal in which a person feels almost or literally dead and, in Jiang's framing, enters the spirit world.
An afterlife review in which one sees and feels everything one did to others.
Jiang's analogy for the priest class that controls rulers while remaining outside the ruler's control.
A psychological theory Jiang presents as the model behind enhanced interrogation: breaking a fighter into passive victimhood.
The official label Jiang uses for torture programs he interprets as psychological experiments.
A CIA-era brainwashing and drug-testing program used by Jiang as the modern continuation of ritual programming.
One of the modern domains Jiang says continues the same control logic used on the pharaoh.
The idea, raised by a student and answered by Jiang, that social media functions as a mechanism of social control through infrastructure rather than only platform ownership.

chronology_notes.note: This lecture explicitly builds on the prior class in the Secret History sequence, reviewing the religion/worldview model before moving to mind control.
The lecture explicitly reuses Monkey Island from an earlier Secret History class as the bridge from myth to programmed inheritance.
This packet sets up the later modern-comparison section by promising that the named Egyptian techniques still appear today.
The lecture turns from ancient Egypt to the War on Terror after summarizing the psychology of dissociative power.
This packet is the explicit modern-history turn: 9/11, Abu Ghraib, CIA interrogation, ISIS, and MK Ultra are brought into the Egyptian framework.
The lecture closes by promising later discussion of mass media, mass education, and mass psychology as continuations of the pharaoh-control model.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR likely renders 'proto-Buddhism' as 'Puro -Buddhism', but the Indus Valley religion-to-Buddhism claim is clear.
ASR renders Apophis as 'Ophiathus'; the serpent-opponent role is clear from context.
ASR garbles the alleged command phrase; the broader claim about trigger-word programming is clear but the phrase should not be treated as exact.
ASR is damaged around the phrase describing the pharaoh as a programmable robot; the priest-control claim remains clear.
ASR is damaged around Abu Zubaydah's name and the transition phrase 'IKEA'; do not quote those words as reliable.
ASR renders 'MK Ultra' as 'NBA ultra' in one place; context establishes the intended term.
ASR likely renders Sidney Gottlieb as 'simon gobley'; avoid quoting the name directly without verification.
The closing disclaimer is part of the source and should accompany any public treatment of the Egyptian reconstruction.
